serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of grilled sea bass spaghetti weighs approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of grilled sea bass fillet over a bed of cooked spaghetti with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cooked Spaghetti (SR Legacy #20124) and Cooked Sea Bass (SR Legacy #15077), combined weighted estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for grilled sea bass spaghetti as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked spaghetti (FDC ID 20124, ~158 kcal/100g, protein 5.8g, fat 0.9g, carb 30.9g) and cooked sea bass (FDC ID 15077, ~124 kcal/100g, protein 23.6g, fat 2.6g, carb 0g), combined in an approximate 55:35 pasta-to-fish ratio with 10% sauce/oil contribution. The input record values (energy 162.5 kcal, protein 11.25g, fat 7.0g, carb 13.75g) are broadly consistent with this blended estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 237.5 mg/100g reflects soy sauce and salt seasoning. All values are estimates for a composite restaurant dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Spaghetti, white, boiled (GI ~46); composite dish estimate weighted by carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled sea bass spaghetti. GI estimated at 45 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white spaghetti (GI ~46 per University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Sea bass and olive oil contribute negligible carbohydrates and lower the effective GI of the composite dish slightly. Glycemic load per 400g serving calculated as: (GI 45 × carbohydrate_g per serving 55g) / 100 = 24.75, rounded to 24.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 237.5 mg/100g; estimated 950 mg per 400g serving, approaching 40% of the 2400 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and salt in preparation; the sea bass and olive oil components are otherwise cardioprotective, but the sodium load from seasoning requires active management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined white spaghetti provides ~55g carbohydrate per 400g serving with only 4g dietary fibre, producing a moderate-to-high glycaemic load of approximately 24.6
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white spaghetti with wholemeal or legume-based pasta to reduce glycaemic load, increase the sea bass portion relative to pasta, and avoid added sugars in the sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.25g/100g; sea bass provides omega-3 fatty acids; olive oil contributes monounsaturated fats; trans fat is negligible at 0.03g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is broadly suitable for cardiovascular health due to its lean fish protein, low saturated fat, and olive oil base; sodium reduction in seasoning is the primary modification recommended for CVD patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from sea bass and oleocanthal from olive oil provide anti-inflammatory benefit; saturated fat is very low
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory profile of grilled sea bass and olive oil; chilli flakes and soy sauce should be minimised, and wholemeal pasta substituted to reduce refined carbohydrate-driven low-grade inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Sea Bass Spaghetti is a Japanese-Western fusion pasta dish served in Singapore, featuring a grilled fillet of sea bass (suzuki) over a bed of boiled white spaghetti, finished with a light pan sauce typically made from olive oil, garlic, soy sauce, lemon juice, and parsley. The dish combines the clean, mild flavour and firm texture of grilled sea bass with the soft, slightly chewy texture of spaghetti, unified by a savoury-umami sauce that reflects Japanese culinary sensibility applied to a Western pasta format. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 11.25g protein, 7.0g fat (saturated fat 1.25g), and 13.75g carbohydrate with 1.0g dietary fibre and 237.5mg sodium. A standard 400g plate delivers roughly 650 kcal and 45g protein, making it a protein-rich meal. The glycaemic index is estimated at 45 (low), driven by white spaghetti as the primary carbohydrate source; however, the glycaemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 24.6 due to serving size. Clinically, the dish is broadly suitable for cardiovascular health given its lean fish protein, low saturated fat, and olive oil base. Sodium content requires monitoring for hypertension patients. For Type 2 Diabetes management, substituting wholemeal or legume-based pasta is recommended to reduce glycaemic load. Sea bass provides omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory relevance for rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is dairy-free and contains gluten, fish, and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked spaghetti and cooked sea bass in an approximate ratio consistent with the dish description. The input record values are broadly plausible and have been retained. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (white spaghetti) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for this composite restaurant dish (grilled sea bass spaghetti) are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. Values would require laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.03g/100g from the input record; this is a trace level consistent with minor residual trans fats from cooking oils and is retained. The dish is sold under the menu code 452D at the originating GrabFood restaurant in Singapore. Primary cuisine classification is Japanese-Western fusion; country_of_origin is Singapore as the dish is a Singapore restaurant creation. Halal status is unconfirmed as soy sauce brand and sourcing are unknown. The input record ethnic_tags (Sushi, Tempura) are not applicable to this dish and have been removed from the output.
